subject: Careers
topic: Bias Mini-Unit — Day 2: Anchoring, Halo Effect, and Framing

worksheet.title: Bias Mini-Unit — Day 2: Anchoring, Halo Effect, and Framing
anchor: Learning goal: I can identify anchoring bias, the halo effect, and framing effect, and I can explain how wording and first impressions change judgment.
Use this worksheet during the mini-lesson, then move into work time on your career presentation.

questions.q_text: Quick start: Why do first impressions matter so much, even when they are incomplete?
Key notes: Anchoring bias means relying too much on the first number, idea, or detail you hear. Write your own example or note.
Key notes: Halo effect means assuming many positive things because of one strong positive trait. Write your own example or note.
Key notes: Framing effect means reacting differently depending on how the same information is presented. Write your own example or note.

Practice: The first salary number a student sees becomes the number they compare everything else to. Bias: ____________________
A job seems good in every way because it is respected. Bias: ____________________
A presenter chooses wording that makes a career sound better even though the facts are the same. Bias: ____________________
Apply it to your career presentation: What is one anchor that could affect how you judge a career?
Apply it to your career presentation: What is one career that sounds impressive but also has real challenges? List both.
Apply it to your career presentation: Rewrite one sentence from your presentation so it is persuasive but still fair.
Exit reflection: Where could framing show up in a career presentation? Give one honest example.

self_check: I can name anchoring bias, halo effect, and framing effect.
I checked whether first impressions or wording could distort my career presentation.
I am ready to continue revising or building my presentation.
